title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several significant items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sewer discharge violations with fines up to $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and construction services for the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout and Bladel Avenue projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
Review 2025 Water Utility PSC Annual Report
Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
Construction Service Proposals - Alderson/Jelinek and Bladel Avenue

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction (Schofield Avenue to Community Center Drive), covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project may be moved to 2028. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Schofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street repairs. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was tentatively scheduled for Thursday, May 28 at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported spring road maintenance including mailbox repairs, sign repairs, and curb line sweeping. Significant flooding issues required dewatering operations on Weston Avenue tributary for approximately a week. Discussion of potential permanent pump solutions for recurring flooding near Crane Meadows area. Culverts failed on Jones Street requiring replacement. Equipment winterization changeover and maintenance was detailed.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee and ongoing well maintenance. Significant infiltration/inflow issues were identified - April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration. The Kathleen lift station showed evidence of sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers. In-pipe technologies contractor will begin INI study to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with prior year's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million due to infrastructure additions including Wells 7 and 8. PAS settlement funds of $1.44 million were received. The utility serves 6,100 customers including 5,250 in Weston and 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution requests state legislature support for long-term transportation funding. Current state funding doesn't adequately cover local road maintenance, snow plowing, and recurring costs, requiring subsidy from general tax dollars. Wisconsin remains relatively affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

Michael presented the recycling program at the Ryan Street site, noting King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton (vs. $4.25 from competitor) for crushing 30,000 tons, totaling $126,900. Analysis showed this saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, representing about $130,000 in savings across this year's projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Following discussion of clear water infiltration issues, staff sought guidance on enforcement fines. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. Committee member Zagami suggested waiting for the INI study results, then sending violation letters with 30-60 day correction periods. Motion passed directing staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100/day.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a duplex developer at 2230 Radkey Avenue needs temporary water/sewer connection since mains don't extend to the lot. Agreement allows long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction, when the owner will pay assessment for main extension. Committee directed that language specify reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously with the 2029 deadline amendment.

Agreement with Village of Rothschild for utility work on Military Road reconstruction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share is $94,860 for sanitary sewer ($6,156) and water main ($88,704) relocation needed due to storm sewer conflicts. Rothschild holds the contract with Steam Construction. Motion to approve passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelinek roundabout, MSA proposed $37,315 vs. Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 vs. Clark Dietz at $73,730; staff recommended Clark Dietz for the additional $3,000 due to having full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern. Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Approved April 13, 2026 meeting minutes
Acknowledged April 2026 water and sewer permits
Scheduled Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Adopted Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Awarded 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated total)
Directed staff to create sewer discharge violation enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day
Approved temporary water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with main extension assessment due no later than 2029
Approved Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for estimated $94,860
Approved MSA for Alderson/Jelinek construction services ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue construction services ($73,730)
Staff to provide cost analysis of infiltration/inflow treatment costs at next meeting
Staff to add street lighting to Schofield Avenue 2030 project scope
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially boosting attendance at the local festival.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair with Jackson providing the second; motion passed. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill from Taste and Glow Balloonfest (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented the grant request. He reported 11,000-12,000 attendees last year and announced that the competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making them the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commissioner Hodal questioned room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request that the organization provide better hotel room data in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting the winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. The candidates visited local attractions including Mullen's Cheese and expressed appreciation for the Marathon County community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest to provide hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with all commissioners present voting in favor.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline easement running through the property. Chief Jeremy explained the building is designed for future growth with capacity for 50-60 officers versus current staffing of 40. An alternate storage shed was discussed as a bid alternate depending on costs. Commissioner Joel asked about landscaping requirements, which Jen confirmed meets current relaxed code. Discussion included ensuring the shed placement allows for future building expansion to the north. The motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jen presented a new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per salt storage facility in wellhead protection areas - a warm weather inspection for structural integrity and a winter operational inspection for material handling practices. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be contacted about compliance. Commissioners emphasized the village's vulnerability due to shallow wells (90-110 feet) and sandy soil. The program was approved unanimously with the amendment to report back to both Plan Commission and Public Works committee.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Approved unanimously without discussion.
Approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Jen announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites. Discussion also occurred about potential new residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with Ehlers having prepared a presentation on qualifications that may be shared with commissioners or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via video call. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the new police department project, referencing previous concerns with the Green Heck Turner building about parking lot islands and landscaping standards. He also requested that the salt storage shed inspection program include annual reporting back to the commission on inspection results and compliance status.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with two annual inspections per facility
Staff to report back to Plan Commission and Public Works committee on inspection program results in approximately one year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility near Sternberg regarding wellhead protection conditional use compliance
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send calendar invites for comprehensive plan steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations added as future agenda item for fall 2026
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8th, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center on their missions, programs, and budgets. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received updates on homelessness response efforts and various county services.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute, ordinance, resolution or policy
Review of standing committee ordinances and committee structure
Health Department overview and budget presentation

discussions.item: Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented an overview of the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect against environmental hazards. She highlighted the department's 37.6 FTE staff working across environmental health, nursing, and community health improvement teams. Suginaka noted new public health vending machines stocked with free tick kits and resources, the Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, and a new homeless system response position created in partnership with the City of Wausau. Supervisor Kremes asked about pandemic preparedness, and Suginaka confirmed comprehensive emergency plans are in place with staff well-trained from COVID-19 experience.

Administrator Lance Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. He explained the department's 140 FTE staff working in child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability, noting many participants had never had a bank account or been on a lease before. He indicated future restructuring requests may come forward due to increased community need.

Veteran Service Officer Erin Galindo reported the office increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE in 2025 and conducted 8,149 veteran interactions, up significantly from previous years. He noted the financial impact of $44 million annually coming to county veterans, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo highlighted partnerships with the Center for Veterans Issues for housing stability and suicide prevention efforts, noting Marathon County had 30 suicides in 2025, seven of which were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional collaboration with neighboring counties, and Galindo explained strong working relationships exist but state statute requires each county to maintain its own veteran service officer.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood) with about 60 FTE staff and 385-400 volunteers. He described services including information assistance, caregiver support, dementia care, falls prevention, and the Meals on Wheels nutrition program. Ray noted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs rising 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The organization had not requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024 due to the efficiency of the regional model. Vice Chair Rosenberg praised the culturally appropriate meals program at the Hmong American Center.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Strategic plan discussion to be added to upcoming meeting agendas
Next meeting confirmed for Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initial incorrect date)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinance amendments including regulations for mobile food vendors and e-bikes, and discussed ongoing concerns about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved various license renewals.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Work product transmittals including building permits and budget status
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment criticizing the lack of detailed cost data for park maintenance. Trustee Olsen supported the fees as justified given population growth. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell and second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermalang and second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Parks committee reported mats installed at Yellow Banks and trees removed from the river. Public Works discussed upcoming contracts for recycling and inspection projects. Tourism committee reported Trustee Olsen elected as vice chair and secretary. The president reported that the MPD Joint Finance committee voted to disperse funds back to the three communities (Schofield, Town of Weston, and Village of Weston) as proceedings wind down over the next 12-18 months.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance creates reciprocal fire inspections for food trucks across multiple municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and others. Once inspected by any participating department, a sticker indicates compliance for all jurisdictions. Approved unanimously with motion by Lope and second by Zagami.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. After discussion, Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy by striking sentences requiring specific health/safety/welfare justifications, making the policy more broad as previously discussed. The amendment passed 5-2 with Trustees Hermalang and Olsen voting no. The original motion as amended also passed 5-2 with the same dissenting votes.

President appointed Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermalang, second by Lope, approved unanimously. The board agreed to include applicant resumes in future packets for transparency.

discussions.item: Secondhand Article and Jewelry Dealer License
discussions.body: The board approved a secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained this involves buying and selling precious metals with significant dollar amounts. An amendment was added requiring satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

The board discussed concerns about the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after being displaced from the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue site. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass and congestion. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors prefer having their vehicles nearby. The board acknowledged parking challenges but noted the market remains popular. Discussion included potential future locations with no decisions made. Renee clarified the market operates at a profit through grants, vendor fees, and token reimbursements.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during the general public comment period criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000, questioning why the engineering technician position remains unfilled, and criticizing the appointment process for the Safer board. He also criticized the lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park funding in the meeting packet. During the park impact fee public hearing, Pitel spoke again questioning the lack of specific cost data justifying the fee increases.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor license ordinance (26-008) approved for reciprocal fire inspections
Standing committees ordinance (26-009) approved
Nuisances ordinance (26-011) approved
E-bike regulations ordinance (26-012) approved
Resolution 2026-014 supporting long-term transportation funding adopted
Remote meeting attendance policy amended with broader attendance provisions
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC pending background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
All license renewals approved for 2026-2027 term
Comprehensive Plan Steering Committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 PM
Staff to bring Weston Avenue speed limit study back to board
Applicant resumes for committee appointments to be included in future board packets

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the at-large Executive Committee positions, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following severe tornado and flood damage in the Ringle area. The board suspended rules to consider the emergency tipping fee reduction, which lowers fees from $65/ton to approximately $36.94/ton for certified storm debris.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution D15 - Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution D16 - Social services budget amendment
Resolution E17 - Landfill tipping fee modification
Public comment on tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, developing new supervisors as leaders, and respecting supervisor preferences. He noted 34% of the board is in first or second terms. The appointments were approved by motion from Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg, who cited her advocacy for children and elders), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved unanimously on motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck.

discussions.item: Resolution D15 - Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Resolution 24-26 was moved by Supervisor Morash and seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution D16 - Social Services Budget Amendment
discussions.body: Resolution 25-26 was moved by Supervisor P and seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution E17 - Landfill Tipping Fee Modification for Disaster Debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Director Hagen Busher presented the emergency resolution from the Solid Waste Management Board regarding reduced tipping fees for tornado and flood debris cleanup in Ringle and surrounding areas. The state waived $13.10/ton, Ringle waived $2.33/ton, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94/ton (covering costs only) from the standard $65/ton gate rate. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were allowed to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely and extend the timeline beyond 60 days. Multiple supervisors discussed concerns about insurance delays, tracking costs, and future policy. The board unanimously suspended rules to consider the resolution, then unanimously approved Resolution 26-26.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty urged the county to waive fees entirely, noting private businesses had donated $40,000+ in cleanup efforts while Marathon County had not provided significant financial assistance. She requested a six-month fee waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of approximately 44 affected homeowners, noting many are still waiting for insurance adjusters and asked for extended relief and fee waivers for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large Executive Committee positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94/ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on disaster-related tipping fees within six months
Staff to track disaster debris separately and require certification that waste is storm-related

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry
Department overview: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made the motion to approve and Supervisor Drebeck seconded. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and conclusions of law, Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the rezoning from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy designation, and Pogle explained the property has a home and horse riding stable, with the rezoning needed to make the home a conforming use. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to individual tornado victims not in declared disaster areas. Supervisor Sefeld questioned if this could lead to future levy increases. Eric Vogel asked about insurance coverage. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then amended to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other permits. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment carried unanimously. The amended motion to forward to county board passed, though not unanimously.

Dave Hagenbusher presented on Solid Waste operations, covering the landfill's 45-year history, tipping fee structure, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, and upcoming $6 million leachate treatment project required by 2032. Tom Lovelin presented on Parks, Recreation and Forestry, noting the department serves both county and city of Wausau with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees. Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Lovelin announced his retirement effective June 1st after 30 years of service.

actionItems: Forward amended fee waiver resolution to county board (waiving only $160 reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by April 17 tornado)
Rezoning for James Offer/David Tract property forwarded to county board for approval
Rezoning for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property forwarded to county board for approval
Joe Tucker selected as new county forest administrator replacing retiring Tom Lovelin
Special educational meeting on conservation topics planned for summer

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center, approved signage for Yellow Banks kayak launches, and received updates on Kennedy Park funding and the disc golf course. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time and Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Kennedy Park update and timeline presentation
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion
Future meeting topics and staff remarks

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes from the previous meeting. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

Director Sean Oster provided a timeline update on the Kennedy Park project, noting the comprehensive plan was approved in November 2023 and the master plan the following month. Scott questioned whether village capital dollars are earmarked for Kennedy Park; Oster confirmed no funds are currently allocated as they were pulled pending the fundraising project. The Friends of Kennedy Park are targeting $117,000 for upper field lights and currently have approximately $85,000 at the community foundation. They plan a public check presentation at their June tournament. The committee chair emphasized giving the volunteer Friends group grace, noting they are not withholding information but planning a public announcement.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and maps showing the location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines for equipment access. Dan explained the mats protect the two gas pipelines during construction work. The committee reviewed photos of mat installation and additional material added for approaches.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the next meeting because Chair Katrina, who requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The resident who spoke during public comment opposing dogs at the pool indicated he may return for next month's discussion.

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete benches and garbage cans along the trail, noting their sandblasting pressure washer cannot access the area due to terrain and narrow bridges. Anti-vandalism coatings and volunteer workdays were discussed as potential solutions. Committee members suggested community volunteers might help with maintenance given the trail's popularity. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan Kroger from Marathon County Parks presented version seven of signs for kayak launches along the river. Three signs will be installed in Village of Weston parks (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge levels, and location-specific history. The Highway J launch may be closed this season due to bridge replacement. A motion to approve the signs and forward to the village board passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds after receiving an inquiry. The committee agreed to bring it back for discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th.

publicComment: Two residents offered public comment. Andy (6504 [address inaudible]) opposed allowing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston) criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as demonstrating 'zero desire for interaction and input from the general public,' questioned the Kennedy Park funding report accuracy, and asked for transparency on funds raised from the glow event and lighting fundraiser.
actionItems: Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti cleaning options for Oaklair River Trail
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs approved and forwarded to village board
Staff to bring food allergy awareness playground signage for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April financial reports showing TIF district bonds remain on track for October payoff. The Municipal Clerk presented an extensive budget overview covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and staffing, while committee member Evan Soaper announced his resignation from the committee due to relocation.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentations - Municipal Clerk's budget overview
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
discussions.body: Jessica from finance reported the TIF district bonds are on track for October payoff. The process will require calling the CDA to order for a resolution to call the bonds, with Ehlers writing a letter to US Bank Trust. A committee member asked if final numbers still look good, and Jessica confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Passed unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented a comprehensive overview of the clerk's office operations. The office has reduced from four full-time staff to two full-time and one part-time (Leah), with plans to add another part-time position. She detailed election administration costs noting postage increased from $6,000 to $15,000 due to fall presidential election demands. Pet licensing was brought back in-house from Pet Data, saving approximately $5,495 annually. She noted the chief inspector wage of $13/hour is below average and will likely increase to $15/hour for the next two-year term. The quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually ($9,000 postage, $20,000 printing), prompting discussion about potentially eliminating printed copies, though previous attempts met resident resistance.

Discussion revealed that neutered dog licensing fees doubled from $10 to $20 at Marathon County's request, as the county was only collecting $65,000 from municipalities while paying $100,000 annually to the Humane Society. A committee member noted the non-dog stray contract cost $175 per animal, including for eight mice picked up last year, prompting discussion about requiring law enforcement to bring in strays rather than allowing residents to do so directly.

Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well over the first three Saturdays, with the market now full. Vendors expressed happiness with the setup and location. Committee members raised concerns about parking on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying parking is now allowed on the west side of Alderson. Discussion noted general parking congestion in the area during concurrent track meets and baseball games.

Evan Soaper announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted there are now multiple open spots on this and other committees, encouraging outreach to interested citizens.

actionItems: Minutes from previous meeting approved
April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th, addressing several ordinance amendments and policy matters. Key actions included recommending approval of nuisance code amendments, an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions added, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned property at 4104 Shore Avenue.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: The committee finalized proposed amendments to Chapter 50 regarding nuisances, including tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet specifications were switched as previously discussed). Committee member Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee discussed the proposed e-bike ordinance for Chapter 82 traffic and vehicles. Chief Hunt advocated strongly for prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles) from roadways, sidewalks, and trails, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. The committee discussed scooters and agreed to leave enforcement discretion to officers rather than adding specific exemptions. Olsen made a motion to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited from roadways, sidewalks, trails, and any public property in the village. Seconded by Sherwanka, motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction window (4 AM to 4 PM, October 31 to May 1) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities, which typically have 5-hour windows. Chief Hunt expressed preference for keeping overnight parking restrictions to help clear roads before plowing begins. Michael from DPW suggested eliminating the even/odd requirement and instead signing specific problem areas (approximately 6-8 locations with multifamily housing). The committee decided to defer to July, asking Michael to identify specific exception areas.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk on Alderson Street between their church and DC Everest school parking lot. Michael estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000-$3,000, while a lighted system would cost $25,000-$30,000. The committee was reluctant to set precedent for paying for requested crosswalks. Olsen made a motion that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the cost of installing the crosswalk, the committee recommends board approval. Seconded by John Sherwanka, motion passed unanimously.

Chief Hunt presented a regional fire inspection program for mobile food vendors involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer. Under this program, any participating department can inspect a food truck and apply a sticker valid for all participating municipalities, eliminating redundant inspections. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Sherwanka made a motion to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Planning staff presented three address corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicowski Street (duplex needing second address 5917), Ross Avenue properties requiring resequencing due to an existing apartment building already addressed as 3909, and 2211 Radkey Avenue needing an even number for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicowski as having improper addressing with eight separate addresses for one building. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin a raze order for an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The property has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and was previously considered for a fire training burn but was deemed too unsafe. The structure was found to be housing chickens when investigated several years ago. Current owners prefer the village proceed with demolition with costs added to the tax roll. Olsen made the motion, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, expressing concerns about the new farmers market location at a park. He stated it was 'a terrible execution' and predicted the grass would be destroyed by summer's end. He questioned whether parking vehicles on grass violates village code and raised concerns about ADA-compliant handicap parking being placed on grass rather than asphalt. Senalt also questioned the need for new fire inspections for food trucks and asked why mobile food vendors couldn't set up independently at the park on non-farmers market days. He criticized the age of some code violation cases, noting some have been on the list for years without visible progress.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended for board approval with tree limb height corrections
E-bike ordinance recommended for board approval with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property added
Overnight parking regulations deferred to July meeting; Michael to identify specific problem areas needing signage
Crosswalk for Alderson Street conditionally recommended if Mount Olive Church pays installation costs; Michael to contact them
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections recommended for board approval
Address corrections for Delicowski Street, Ross Avenue, and Radkey Avenue recommended for board approval
Raze order for 4104 Shore Avenue authorized to proceed
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber for potential code violations

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
